title: Elder Nun Subhā: Here, people are lustful, Heedless, with defiled minds, They oppose each other as enemies, They struggle in disputes. Killing, binding, torturing, Losing…
content:
Elder Nun Subhā:

Here, people are lustful,
Heedless, with defiled minds,
They oppose each other as enemies,
They struggle in disputes.

Killing, binding, torturing,
Losing wealth, grieving, worrying —
Those who sink in sensual pleasures
Meet many calamities.

Why do my relatives,
Like my enemies,
Bind me into the circle of sensual pleasures?
Know that I have gone forth
Because I see that sensual pleasures are fearful.

Silver and gold cannot
Destroy the defilements.
Sensual pleasures are enemies,
Murderers, killers.
They are adversaries,
Darts, bonds.

Why do my relatives,
Like my enemies,
Bind me into the circle of sensual pleasures?
Know that I have gone forth —
Shaved my head, wearing the great robe.

Living on food
Accumulated by alms-round,
Robes made from discarded rags —
Living thus is suitable for me,
The basic life of homelessness.

Great sages have abandoned
Sensual desires, both divine and human.
Dwelling secure, liberated,
Unshaken, at ease.

Do not lead me to sensual desires —
There is no safety in sensual desires.
Sensual desires are enemies, murderers,
Producing suffering, like a heap of burning coals.

The path is dangerous, full of fear,
Full of suffering, with thorns —
The path of craving is rugged,
The path of delusion is vast.

Filled with terrifying calamities,
Sensual desires are like a snake’s head —
Fools delight in sensual desires,
Blind worldlings.

Beings sink in the muddy swamp —
Those who do not see the world,
They do not know
The end of birth and death.

People, because of sensual desires,
Go to evil destinations —
Most go that way,
Making themselves sick.

Thus, sensual desires produce enmity,
They burn, they defile.
Sensual desires belong to the body —
They bind people to death.

Sensual desires make one insane,
They cause disordered speech and mind,
They defile beings,
About to fall into Māra’s trap.

Sensual desires are endlessly perilous,
Full of suffering, a great poison —
Little sweetness, they create strife —
They wither the bright day.

Thus I decide —
I will not turn back because of sensual desires,
Which cause misfortune —
I will always delight in Nibbāna.

Fighting against sensual desires,
I wait, cool, calm —
I will live heedfully,
Destroying the fetters of sensual desire.

I follow that path
That the great sages have walked —
Sorrowless, stainless,
Safe, straight — the Eightfold Path.

See this Subhā,
Established in the true Dhamma —
A goldsmith’s daughter,
Attaining freedom from sensual desire,
Sitting in meditation,
At the root of a tree.

title: “Monks, just as in the last month of the rainy season, in autumn, when the rice has ripened, a cowherd tending cows would poke the…
content: “Monks, just as in the last month of the rainy season, in autumn, when the rice has ripened, a cowherd tending cows would poke the cows on this side and that with a stick, restraining and checking them. Why is that? Because the cowherd sees that otherwise there would be death, imprisonment, beating, or blame. Monks, even so, I saw the peril, the degradation, the defilement of unwholesome states, and I saw the escape, the benefit, the purification of wholesome states.”

title: “A person intoxicated by craving Throws himself into the stream Like a spider into its own web. But the wise cut it off, Having abandoned…
content:
“A person intoxicated by craving
Throws himself into the stream
Like a spider into its own web.
But the wise cut it off,
Having abandoned all suffering, they wander on without desire.”

title: “Bound with sinews and bones, Plastered with flesh and skin, This body is covered by skin — Truth is not seen as it is. Within,…
content:
“Bound with sinews and bones,
Plastered with flesh and skin,
This body is covered by skin —
Truth is not seen as it is.

Within, it is full of liver, bladder,
Heart, lungs,
Kidneys and spleen,
Nasal mucus, saliva, sweat, fat,
Blood, synovial fluid, bile, and lymph.

From these nine streams,
Impurity constantly flows —
From the eyes, discharge flows,
From the ears, discharge flows.

From the nostrils, mucus flows,
From the mouth, sometimes vomit —
Bile and phlegm flow —
From the body, sweat and dirt.

In the hollow of the head,
The brain is full.
The fool, led by ignorance,
Thinks it is beautiful.

When it lies dead, swollen,
Livid, discarded in a charnel ground,
Relatives no longer care.

Dogs, jackals, wolves, worms,
Crows, vultures, and other creatures eat it.”

title: “Monks, the charioteer, obeying the prince, drove to a person who had just died. Monks, when he saw the corpse of a dead person, Prince…
content: “Monks, the charioteer, obeying the prince, drove to a person who had just died. Monks, when he saw the corpse of a dead person, Prince Vipassī asked: ‘Good man, why is this person called dead?’ ‘Your Highness, he is called dead because now his mother, father, and other blood relatives no longer see him; and he no longer sees his mother, father, or other blood relatives.’ ‘Good man, then am I not dead — will I be subject to death? Will my father, mother, or other blood relatives not see me? And will I not see my father, mother, or other blood relatives?’ ‘Your Highness, you and I as well — even though we are not yet dead, we will die. Your father, mother, and other blood relatives will not see you, and you will not see your father, mother, or other blood relatives.’ ‘Good man, then this visit to the pleasure garden is enough. Drive back to the palace.’ ‘Yes, Your Highness.’ Monks, the charioteer, obeying the prince, drove back to the palace. Monks, Prince Vipassī returned to the palace, suffering, grieving, and thought: ‘Shameful indeed is this birth! And anyone who is born must age, must be sick, must die like this!’”

title: “Good sirs, if, while reflecting, a monk sees clearly that all unarisen evil, unwholesome states are not yet abandoned within him, then he should arouse…
content: “Good sirs, if, while reflecting, a monk sees clearly that all unarisen evil, unwholesome states are not yet abandoned within him, then he should arouse energy to abandon those evil, unwholesome states. Good sirs, if, while reflecting, a monk sees clearly that all evil, unwholesome states have been abandoned within him, then he should dwell with joyful mind, training in wholesome states day and night.”

title: “Monk, when a person dwells with sense faculties guarded, with moderation in eating, committed to wakefulness, watching over wholesome states, devoted to developing the factors…
content: “Monk, when a person dwells with sense faculties guarded, with moderation in eating, committed to wakefulness, watching over wholesome states, devoted to developing the factors of enlightenment at the beginning and end of the night — then his body does not feel intoxicated, his eye sees directions clearly, the Dhamma is remembered by him. Sloth and torpor do not overcome his mind. With gladness, he lives the holy life. He has no doubts about wholesome states. Therefore, monks, you should train thus: ‘I will guard the sense faculties … be devoted to developing the factors of enlightenment at the beginning and end of the night.’”

title: “And how, monks, is a monk one who does not bind up the wound? Here, monks, when a monk sees a form with the eye,…
content: “And how, monks, is a monk one who does not bind up the wound? Here, monks, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he grasps the general feature, grasps the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—he does not restrain that cause, does not guard the eye, does not practice restraint of the eye. When he hears a sound with the ear … smells an odor with the nose … tastes a flavor with the tongue … feels a touch with the body … cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, he grasps the general feature, grasps the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—he does not restrain that cause, does not guard the mind, does not practice restraint of the mind. Thus, monks, is a monk who does not bind up the wound.”

title: “And what, monks, are the things that should be fully understood? Form, monks, is a thing that should be fully understood; feeling … perception ……
content: “And what, monks, are the things that should be fully understood? Form, monks, is a thing that should be fully understood; feeling … perception … formations … consciousness is a thing that should be fully understood. These, monks, are the things that should be fully understood. And what, monks, is full understanding? The destruction of greed, the destruction of hatred, the destruction of delusion — this, monks, is called full understanding.”

title: “Monks, there are these four ignoble searches. What four? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to aging seeks what is subject to aging; being…
content: “Monks, there are these four ignoble searches. What four? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to aging seeks what is subject to aging; being himself subject to sickness seeks what is subject to sickness; being himself subject to death seeks what is subject to death; being himself subject to defilement seeks what is subject to defilement. These, monks, are the four ignoble searches.”
